---
title: "Structural Identifiability via Lie Derivatives: Two Examples"
author: "Simon Beyer"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

# Background

A parameter $\theta$ is **structurally non-identifiable** when the model produces
the same output for a whole family of parameter values, so that no amount of
noise-free data can tell those values apart. We detect non-identifiabilities via
the **observability-identifiability matrix** (OIM).

## The Lie Derivative

Given a vector field $f(x)$ and scalar function $\phi(x)$, the Lie derivative is:

$$L_f \phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \cdot f(x) = \sum_i \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_i} f_i(x)$$

This is the time derivative of $\phi$ along system trajectories,
$L_f \phi = \frac{d\phi}{dt}\big|_{\dot x = f}$,
and higher orders follow recursively: $L_f^k \phi = L_f(L_f^{k-1}\phi)$.

## The Observability-Identifiability Matrix

For a system $\dot x = f(x,\theta)$, $y = g(x,\theta)$, the output curve near
$t = t_0$ is fully described by the Taylor coefficients
$(g,\, L_f g,\, L_f^2 g, \ldots)$ evaluated at $x(t_0)$.
Collecting their gradients with respect to $\theta$ gives the OIM:

$$\mathcal{O}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix}
\partial_\theta\, g \\
\partial_\theta\, L_f g \\
\partial_\theta\, L_f^2 g \\
\vdots
\end{pmatrix}\Bigg|_{x = x(t_0)}$$

**Rank condition:** $\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = \dim\theta$ if and only if
the model is locally structurally identifiable. The null space $\ker(\mathcal{O})$
gives the non-identifiable directions. Rows are added until the rank stops growing;
at most $n + q - 1$ rows are ever needed ($n$ states, $q$ parameters).

## Scaling Symmetries and Their Generators

A **symmetry** is a smooth one-parameter family of transformations
$\Phi_\varepsilon\colon\theta\mapsto\theta(\varepsilon)$ that leaves
$g(x(t),\theta)$ unchanged for all $t$. Its **infinitesimal generator** is

$$\xi(\theta) = \frac{d}{d\varepsilon}\Phi_\varepsilon(\theta)\Big|_{\varepsilon=0}$$

which is a null vector of $\mathcal{O}$: $\mathcal{O}\,\xi = 0$.
The finite orbit is recovered by integrating $\dot\theta = \xi(\theta)$.

A **scaling (toric) symmetry** has the monomial form
$\theta_i(\varepsilon) = \lambda^{c_i}\theta_i(0)$, so its generator is

$$\xi = \sum_i c_i\,\theta_i\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i}$$

with constant integer exponents $c_i$.
Each component $\xi_i \propto \theta_i$ is a monomial — scaling symmetries
can therefore be found by pure integer linear algebra on the monomial-exponent
matrix, without any point evaluation.

A **non-scaling symmetry** has generator components that are general rational
functions of $\theta$. It cannot be detected by the integer kernel alone and
requires the full OIM null space or the Lie symmetry search.

---

# Example 1: Scaling Non-Identifiability (MM Transcription–Translation)

## Model

A transcription factor $x_1$ is introduced as a bolus and drives
Michaelis–Menten transcription of mRNA $x_2$, which is translated into
protein $x_3$ measured with unknown gain:

$$\dot x_1 = -k_1 x_1$$
$$\dot x_2 = \frac{v\, x_1}{K_m + x_1} - k_2\, x_2$$
$$\dot x_3 = k_3\, x_2 - k_4\, x_3$$
$$g = \mathrm{scale} \cdot x_3$$

**Parameters:** $\theta = (v,\, k_1,\, k_2,\, k_3,\, k_4,\, K_m,\, \mathrm{scale})^\top$

**Initial condition:** $x_1(t_0) = \delta$ (bolus), $x_2(t_0) = x_3(t_0) = 0$.

## Lie Derivatives at $t_0$

Let $\alpha = \delta/(K_m+\delta)$.
Since $x_2(t_0) = x_3(t_0) = 0$:

$$L_f^0 g\big|_0 = 0, \qquad L_f^1 g\big|_0 = 0$$

$$L_f^2 g\big|_0 = \mathrm{scale}\cdot k_3\cdot v\alpha$$

$$L_f^3 g\big|_0 = \mathrm{scale}\cdot k_3\cdot v\alpha
                  \left(-\frac{k_1 K_m}{K_m+\delta} - k_2 - k_4\right)$$

Higher derivatives produce new information; the rank saturates after
$L_f^7 g$ (verified numerically via finite-field arithmetic).

## Rank Analysis

| Lie order $k$ | $\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal O)$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0--1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 2 |
| 4 | 3 |
| 5 | 4 |
| 6 | 5 |
| 7 | **5** (saturated) |

$\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal O) = 5 < 7$: null space has **dimension 2**.

## Non-Identifiable Directions

$$\xi_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -v/k_3 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad
\xi_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -v/\mathrm{scale} \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

in the order $(v,\, k_1,\, k_2,\, k_3,\, k_4,\, K_m,\, \mathrm{scale})$.

Both are **scaling symmetries**: the components are monomials in $\theta$.
Integrating $\dot\theta = \xi_1$ gives the orbit
$v \mapsto \lambda^{-1}v$, $k_3 \mapsto \lambda k_3$ (all others fixed):
the mRNA-to-protein flux and the maximal transcription rate trade against
each other at fixed product $v k_3$.
Similarly $\xi_2$ generates $v \mapsto \mu^{-1}v$,
$\mathrm{scale} \mapsto \mu\,\mathrm{scale}$.

Identifiable combinations:

$$\boxed{\psi_1 = v\cdot k_3, \qquad \psi_2 = v\cdot\mathrm{scale}}$$

$k_1,\, k_2,\, k_4,\, K_m$ are individually identifiable.

## The Non-Identifiable Manifold

The two symmetries act simultaneously. Integrating $\dot\theta = \lambda\xi_1 + \mu\xi_2$
gives the explicit two-parameter family of output-equivalent parameter vectors:

$$\mathcal{M}(\psi_1, \psi_2, k_1, k_2, k_4, K_m) =
\left\{\, \theta \;\middle|\;
v = \frac{\psi_1}{k_3} = \frac{\psi_2}{\mathrm{scale}},\quad k_3 > 0
\,\right\}$$

Choosing $v > 0$ as the free parameter, the manifold is the **1D curve**

$$v \;\mapsto\; \begin{pmatrix} v \\ k_1 \\ k_2 \\ \psi_1/v \\ k_4 \\ K_m \\ \psi_2/v \end{pmatrix}, \qquad v \in (0,\infty)$$

**Positivity** is automatically preserved: $k_3 = \psi_1/v > 0$ and
$\mathrm{scale} = \psi_2/v > 0$ for all $v > 0$. Every point on this
hyperbolic curve produces exactly the same output trajectory.

## Reparametrisation

Fix one representative per equivalence class by setting $v = 1$
(normalise the maximal transcription rate). Then:

$$\tilde k_3 = \psi_1 = k_3\big|_{v=1}, \qquad
  \widetilde{\mathrm{scale}} = \psi_2 = \mathrm{scale}\big|_{v=1}$$

The reduced model with $\theta_{\rm red} = (\tilde k_3,\, k_1,\, k_2,\, k_4,\, K_m,\, \widetilde{\mathrm{scale}})^\top$
is locally identifiable. Positivity holds for all $\tilde k_3, \widetilde{\mathrm{scale}} > 0$.

---

# Example 2: Non-Scaling Non-Identifiability (Catenary Two-Compartment Model)

## Model

A **catenary compartment model** describes exchange of a substance between
two connected compartments with first-order transfer rates in both directions —
the name refers to the chain-like topology $x_1 \leftrightarrow x_2$, as opposed
to a mammillary arrangement. Such models are standard in pharmacokinetics to
describe drug distribution between plasma and tissue.

$$\dot x_1 = -k_1 x_1 + k_2 x_2$$
$$\dot x_2 = k_3 x_1 - k_4 x_2$$
$$g = x_1 + x_2$$

**Parameters:** $\theta = (k_1,\, k_2,\, k_3,\, k_4)^\top$

**Initial condition:** $x_1(t_0) = \delta$, $x_2(t_0) = 0$.

Only the **total amount** $x_1 + x_2$ is observed.

## Lie Derivatives at $t_0$

**$L_f^0 g\big|_0 = \delta$**

**$L_f^1 g\big|_0$:**

$$L_f^1 g = f_1 + f_2 = (k_3-k_1)x_1 + (k_2-k_4)x_2
\xrightarrow{\;t_0\;} \delta(k_3-k_1)$$

**$L_f^2 g\big|_0$:**

$$L_f^2 g\big|_0 = \delta\bigl[k_1(k_1-k_3) + k_3(k_2-k_4)\bigr]$$

**$L_f^3 g\big|_0$:**

$$L_f^3 g\big|_0 = \delta\bigl[-k_1\bigl(k_1(k_1-k_3)+k_3(k_2-k_4)\bigr)
                              - k_3\bigl(k_2(k_1-k_3)+k_4(k_2-k_4)\bigr)\bigr]$$

## Observability-Identifiability Matrix

$$\mathcal O = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
-\delta & 0 & \delta & 0 \\
\delta(2k_1-k_3) & \delta k_3 & \delta(k_2-k_4-k_1) & -\delta k_3 \\
\star & \star & \star & \star
\end{pmatrix}$$

| Lie order $k$ | $\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal O)$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 |
| 3 | 3 |
| 4 | **3** (saturated) |

Null space has **dimension 1**.

## Non-Identifiable Direction and Its Orbit

Solving $\mathcal O\,\xi = 0$:

$$\xi = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ (k_1+k_2-k_4)/k_3 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

The $k_2$-component is a rational function of $\theta$ — this is a
**non-scaling symmetry**. Integrating $\dot\theta = \xi$ gives the orbit:

$$k_1(\varepsilon) = k_1 - \varepsilon, \quad
k_3(\varepsilon) = k_3 - \varepsilon, \quad
k_4(\varepsilon) = k_4 + \varepsilon$$

$$k_2(\varepsilon) = k_2 + \varepsilon\,\frac{-k_1 - k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{k_3}
                    + \varepsilon^2(\ldots)$$

(solved from the linear ODE $\dot k_2 = (k_1+k_2-k_4)/k_3$ with
$k_3 = k_3 - \varepsilon$).

The true invariants — functions constant along the orbit — are found by
inspection:

$$\psi_1 = k_1 - k_3, \qquad \psi_2 = k_3 + k_4$$

since $\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}(k_1-k_3) = -1-(-1) = 0$ and
$\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}(k_3+k_4) = -1+1 = 0$.
The remaining free direction is $k_3$ itself.

## The Non-Identifiable Manifold

Integrating $\dot\theta = \xi$ gives the orbit

$$k_1(\varepsilon) = k_1 - \varepsilon, \quad
k_3(\varepsilon) = k_3 - \varepsilon, \quad
k_4(\varepsilon) = k_4 + \varepsilon, \quad
k_2(\varepsilon) = k_2 + \varepsilon\,\frac{-k_1 - k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4}{k_3} + O(\varepsilon^2)$$

The invariants $\psi_1 = k_1 - k_3$ and $\psi_2 = k_3 + k_4$ are constant along
the orbit since $d(k_1-k_3)/d\varepsilon = -1-(-1) = 0$ and
$d(k_3+k_4)/d\varepsilon = -1+1 = 0$.
Using $k_3$ as the free parameter, the **non-identifiable manifold** is the
1D curve

$$k_3 \;\mapsto\;
\begin{pmatrix} \psi_1 + k_3 \\ k_2 \\ k_3 \\ \psi_2 - k_3 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad k_3 \in \bigl(\max(0,\,-\psi_1),\; \psi_2\bigr)$$

**Positivity constraints** determine the admissible range:

| Parameter | Expression | Positive when |
|---|---|---|
| $k_1$ | $\psi_1 + k_3$ | $k_3 > -\psi_1$ |
| $k_3$ | $k_3$ | $k_3 > 0$ |
| $k_4$ | $\psi_2 - k_3$ | $k_3 < \psi_2$ |
| $k_2$ | $k_2$ (invariant) | always |

So $k_3 \in (\max(0,-\psi_1),\, \psi_2)$, a **bounded open interval** — unlike the
scaling case where the free parameter ranged over all of $(0,\infty)$.
The manifold is a **line segment** in $\mathbb{R}^4$, not a hyperbola.

## Reparametrisation

Fix $k_3 = 1$ (one representative per equivalence class). Then:

$$k_1 = \psi_1 + 1, \quad k_2 = k_2, \quad k_3 = 1, \quad k_4 = \psi_2 - 1$$

Positivity requires $\psi_1 > -1$ and $\psi_2 > 1$.
The reduced model with $\theta_{\rm red} = (\psi_1,\, k_2,\, \psi_2)^\top$
is locally identifiable (full OIM rank verified).

## Contrast with Example 1

| Property | Example 1 (MM cascade) | Example 2 (catenary) |
|---|---|---|
| Null space dimension | 2 | 1 |
| Symmetry type | **scaling** | **non-scaling** |
| Generator components | monomials $\propto \theta_i$ | rational functions of $\theta$ |
| Fast integer kernel | ✓ sufficient | ✗ misses this direction |
| Identifiable combinations | products $vk_3$, $v\cdot\mathrm{scale}$ | differences $k_1-k_3$, $k_3+k_4$ |
| Reparametrisation | fix scale and $k_3$, rename $v$ | fix $k_3=1$, shift remaining rates |

The non-scaling direction in Example 2 cannot be found by
`method = "scaling"` in `symmetryDetection()`; it requires
`method = "observability"` or `method = "liesym"`.
